pattern: Interleave a list with its own reverse by splitting at the middle, reversing the back half, then weaving the two halves together one node at a time.
hints: The target order alternates front, back, front-1, back-1, ... — that is exactly the list zipped together with its own reversal, so think in terms of two halves rather than random jumps.
Find the midpoint with slow/fast pointers, detach and reverse the second half, then merge the first half and the reversed second half by alternating their nodes.
Splice carefully: from the two half-heads, repeatedly link a front node then a back node, advancing each; terminate the final node's next so the list does not loop.

edge_inputs.description: even length weaves front and reversed back
odd length leaves the middle node last
duplicate values must keep node identity, not collapse

stress.description: long random list to confirm midpoint split, reversal, and weave

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def reorderList(self, head):
        nodes = []
        node = head
        while node:
            nodes.append(node)
            node = node.next
        i, j = 0, len(nodes) - 1
        dummy = ListNode(0)
        tail = dummy
        front = True
        while i <= j:
            if front:
                tail.next = nodes[i]
                i += 1
            else:
                tail.next = nodes[j]
                j -= 1
            tail = tail.next
            front = not front
        tail.next = None

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def reorderList(self, head):
        if not head or not head.next:
            return
        slow, fast = head, head
        while fast.next and fast.next.next:
            slow = slow.next
            fast = fast.next.next
        second = slow.next
        slow.next = None
        prev = None
        while second:
            nxt = second.next
            second.next = prev
            prev = second
            second = nxt
        first = head
        while prev:
            n1 = first.next
            n2 = prev.next
            first.next = prev
            prev.next = n1
            first = n1
            prev = n2
